ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general evaporated milk entry (FOODON_03301263); no specific reduced-fat variant code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LX covers evaporated milk generically; no reduced-fat sub-code available. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for reduced-fat evaporated milk; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of evaporated milk is approximately 2 tablespoons (30 g), commonly used as a dairy whitener in hot beverages such as coffee or tea, or as a cooking ingredient in soups and desserts.

nutrient_source.title: Evaporated milk, reduced fat — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for evaporated milk, reduced fat (cr_id F01070004). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01098 (Evaporated milk, lowfat, canned) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for reduced-fat evaporated milk in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 30 is estimated by analogy to full-fat evaporated milk and low-fat milk (GI ~27–32 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Lactose is the primary sugar (GI ~46), but the high protein content (7.8 g/100g) significantly attenuates glycaemic response. GL per 30 g serving = (30 × 10.6/100) × 30/100 = 0.95, rounded to 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (104 mg/100g) with high potassium (348 mg/100g) and calcium (266 mg/100g) supporting DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Reduced-fat evaporated milk is suitable for hypertensive individuals in controlled portions (≤30 g per serve) due to its low sodium and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio of 3.3:1, consistent with DASH dietary targets; avoid adding sugar or high-sodium flavourings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 10.6 g/100g is entirely naturally occurring lactose (GI ~30 by analogy); high protein (7.8 g/100g) moderates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may use reduced-fat evaporated milk in small portions (≤30 g) as a dairy whitener, as its low estimated GI (~30) and high protein content attenuate glycaemic impact; avoid sweetening and monitor cumulative carbohydrate load across the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.3 g/100g), low cholesterol (10 mg/100g), and high calcium (266 mg/100g) with a favourable fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Reduced-fat evaporated milk presents a low cardiovascular risk profile with saturated fat well below the 5 g/100g threshold and cholesterol at 10 mg/100g; it may be used as a lower-fat alternative to full-fat evaporated milk or cream in cooking, consistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium (266 mg/100g) supports bone health in RA patients on corticosteroids; low saturated fat (~1.3 g/100g) is below the 5 g anti-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Reduced-fat evaporated milk is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as a calcium-rich dairy source with low saturated fat; ensure adequate vitamin D intake from other sources to maximise calcium absorption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Evaporated milk (reduced fat) is a shelf-stable canned dairy product produced by heating low-fat cow's milk under vacuum to remove approximately 60% of its water content, resulting in a concentrated, slightly caramelised, creamy liquid with a mildly sweet flavour derived entirely from naturally occurring lactose. Unlike sweetened condensed milk, it contains no added sugar. The reduced-fat variant contains approximately 2.1 g fat per 100 g compared to 8–9 g in full-fat versions, making it a lighter alternative for cooking and beverage use. It is widely used across Southeast Asia, particularly in Singapore and Malaysia, as a dairy whitener for kopi (local coffee) and teh tarik (pulled tea), and as a cooking base for soups, curries, and desserts. Nutritionally, it provides 91 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 2.1 g fat, and 10.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with notably high calcium (266 mg/100g) and potassium (348 mg/100g). Its estimated glycaemic index of approximately 30 (low GI) reflects the attenuating effect of high protein on lactose absorption. Clinically, it is suitable for hypertensive individuals due to its low sodium and high potassium-to-sodium ratio, and appropriate in small portions for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease risk.

meal_context: dairy whitener for kopi and teh tarik
cooking base for soups and curries
substitute for cream in savoury dishes

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01070004, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01098 (evaporated milk, lowfat, canned) as the closest approved-source analogue. GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database data for low-fat milk and evaporated milk. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.3 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat (0.6 g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat (0.1 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01098 (Evaporated milk, lowfat, canned) as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid breakdown for this item; values are consistent with the total fat of 2.1 g/100g reported by HPB. 2. Trans fat set to 0.0 g/100g: confirmed negligible in low-fat dairy products per USDA FoodData Central. 3. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100g: confirmed zero — dairy products contain no dietary fibre. 4. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB or USDA for this specific product): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA NDB 01098 provides partial vitamin data but is not an exact match for this reduced-fat formulation, so -1 is used to avoid misattribution. 5. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on low-fat milk and evaporated milk data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for reduced-fat evaporated milk was found. 6. The input health_suitability entries for diabetes and hypertension incorrectly marked suitable as false despite the nutritional profile supporting conditional suitability; these have been corrected to reflect evidence-based clinical guidance in health_context. 7. The input dietary_tags included 'high_sugar_warning' which is misleading as all sugar is naturally occurring lactose with no added sugar; this tag has been omitted from the output. 8. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301263 and FoodEx2 A00LX are closest parent matches for evaporated milk generically; no reduced-fat specific codes exist in either system.
